claim.hypothesis: Comparing OS and TS thus allows testing whether strategic equivalence translates into behavioral equivalence.
claim.statement: Behavior in the first stage of TS differs strongly both from the one-stage treatment and from theoretical predictions. In particular, subjects exert significantly higher efforts in the first stage of the two-stage tournament.

method.description: The authors conduct a tournament style laboratory experiment, where they compare effort in a multi-stage tournament game with a one-stage tournament game.
method.steps: (1) recruit participants using ORSEE; (2) seat participants in separate cubicles and give them detailed written instructions with neutral framing; (3) the subjects then completed control questionnaires; (4) the experiment then started where subjects entered their effort levels and beliefs about others’ effort levels (this happened in two stages for the multi-level treatment); (5) at the end, participants were informed about their earnings and filled out an exit questionnaire; (6) the experimental results are then analyzed by comparing the different treatments,

results.summary: subjects behave much more competitively in the multistage tournament, exerting efforts that are almost 20% higher than those of their counterparts in the OS treatment.

metadata.original_paper_title: Promotions and Incentives: The Case of Multistage Elimination Tournaments.
